title: Waved bouquet marbling
subtitle: The floral pattern with an added wave pass
excerpt: The waved bouquet is the standard bouquet pattern followed by a gentle wave comb pass that introduces a fluid movement into the flower-cluster shapes. The result has the floral character of the bouquet but with longer, flowing curves that suggest movement. It takes three comb passes after the initial colour drops.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894), public domain. Halfer documents the waved bouquet as a distinct pattern in his catalogue of European marbling traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A marbled pattern created by combing a straight-raked pattern in one direction, then using a fine comb in gentle curves to create flower-like clusters of colour. The bouquet was one of the most popular European bookbinding marbling patterns from the eighteenth century.

content.text: creates defined flower clusters with a comb pass in one direction followed by a floral sweep in the other. The waved variant adds a third pass: a slow lateral sinusoidal movement of the comb that introduces wave-curves into the existing clusters. This must be done immediately after the bouquet comb pass, before the pigment on the surface has started to blend.

attrs.heading: Safety: marbling chemistry
attrs.body: Carrageenan, acrylic pigments, and ox-gall are non-toxic at marbling concentrations. Dispose of used size and rinse water as per local drain guidelines: dilute heavily before disposal. Wash all tools and hands immediately after the session.

items.name: Carrageenan size (slug: carrageenan)
items.description: Prepared the day before. Surface must be clear before use.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid in 3-5 colours (slug: marbling-acrylic-liquid)
items.description: Each prepared with ox-gall. The waved bouquet looks best with colours in a gradient: from dark to light, or from cool to warm.

content.text: Drop the colours in horizontal stripes across the tray. Draw the wide comb (30-35 mm tine spacing) across the tray from left to right in one pass, pulling the stripes into parallel vertical lines of alternating colour.

Use the fine comb (10-12 mm tine spacing). Starting from the top of the tray, draw the comb downward but in a series of gentle S-curves rather than a straight line: the comb tines follow a curving path across the vertical stripes, creating flower-shaped distortions where each curve sweeps through. Move the comb at a consistent pace; stopping mid-pass leaves a straight section among the curves.

Immediately after the bouquet pass, draw the wide comb from left to right. Move it in a slow sinusoidal wave. The tines trace a vertical path: approximately 50 mm amplitude, 100 mm wavelength. This bends and elongates the existing bouquet clusters. The resulting pattern has both the circular character of the bouquet and a flowing lateral movement.

Work quickly after the third pass; the waved bouquet degrades quickly as the pigments begin to blend. Lower the mordanted paper face-down and lift after 30 seconds. Rinse and hang to dry. The waved bouquet is one of the more demanding patterns to reproduce consistently; practise the three-pass sequence on scrap paper before committing to mordanted sheets.
